---
title: "Web application front end"
---

With Propagate, the Owner can build a web application enabling Requesters to:

1. Start a new request or continue an existing one.
2. Fill out the basic information about the project.
3. See all the fields available and select the fields needed for their project.
4. Select filters for e.g., excluding rows with particular values.
5. Review the request.
6. Select the email address of (one of the) Owners to send the request to.

The web application's front end consists of these six "steps" that are described
in detail below.

At each step, except Step 1, it is possible to save the in-progress request by
clicking a "Save" button.

<!-- TODO: Can we include an autosave feature, so all is not lost, if a Requester forgets to save? -->

## Step 1: New or existing request?

In this first step, the Requester is presented with two options: "New request"
or "Load existing request".

Clicking "New request" takes the Requester to the next step. Clicking "Load
existing request" will open a file picker for the Requester to locate the
request they want to load. When the `request.yaml` has been selected and loaded,
Propagate pre-populates the forms in Steps 2-4. The Requester can then continue
throughout the steps and edit as needed.

## Step 2: Project overview

In this step, the Requester fills in the name of their project, the rationale or
motivation for the project as well as their own name and email address.

All these fields are free-text fields, with some basic checks (e.g., email
format).

<!-- TODO: Include a length limit for these texts? Especially thinking of the rationale/motivation -->

## Step 3: Field selection

Here, the Requester gets an overview of the available resources and the fields
in each resource. To select fields, the Requester clicks checkboxes. The fields
are shown in a table with the following columns:

- Selection check box
- Resource name
  <!-- TODO: Omitted the resource title to keep the table less busy, but do we want include it? -->
- Field (name and title)
- Field data type
- Field description

<!-- TODO: Do we want to include the resource description? If yes, how? -->

Using a part of the example `datapackage.json`,
[Flora](https://github.com/seedcase-project/seedcase-soil/blob/main/src/seedcase_soil/example-datapackages/flora.json),
the field selection table (embedded here without any interactivity or any of the
advanced features described below) looks like:

|     | Resource           | Field                                    | Data type | Description                                   |
|-----|--------------------|------------------------------------------|-----------|-----------------------------------------------|
| ☐   | `species_catalog`  | `species_id` <br> Species ID             | integer   | The unique identifier for each species.       |
| ☐   | `species_catalog`  | `scientific_name` <br> Scientific name   | string    | The Latin name of the species.                |
| ☐   | `species_catalog`  | `common_name` <br> Common name           | string    | The common name of the species.               |
| ☐   | `location_catalog` | `region` <br> Region                     | string    | The region the location is in.                |
| ☐   | `location_catalog` | `country` <br> Country                   | string    | The country the location is in.               |
| ☐   | `growth_records`   | `record_id` <br> Record ID               | integer   | The unique identifier for each growth record. |
| ☐   | `growth_records`   | `species_id` <br> Species                | integer   | The species the observation was made for.     |
| ☐   | `growth_records`   | `observation_date` <br> Observation date | date      | The date when the observation was made.       |
| ☐   | `growth_records`   | `growth_stage` <br> Growth Stage         | string    | The observed growth stage of the species.     |

This step also includes the ability to:

- Search and filter fields (shown as a search bar at the top of the table)
- Show all fields or only selected fields (check box at the top of the table)
- Select multiple fields (`Shift+click` to select a range of fields,
  `Ctrl+click`/`Cmd+click` to select non-adjacent fields)

<!-- TODO: click (or potentially hover) on fields to get more information about them? -->
<!-- TODO: if we want this to work nicely with Flower, we could keep this table relatively simple and include links to data package documentation made with Flower? -->

## Step 4: Filter conditions

Now that the relevant fields have been selected, the Requester can apply filters
to them. Leaving this step empty is valid; the request will then include all
rows for the selected fields.

The filter user interface is a visual query builder, where each filter condition
is one row. Each row consists of:

- A field dropdown, only including the fields selected in Step 3.
- An operator dropdown, adapted to the field's data type:
  - String fields: `==`, `!=`, `contains`, `starts with`, and `ends with`
  - Integer and number fields: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, and `>=`
  - Date fields: `==`, `!=`, `before`, `after`, and `between`
- A value input adapted to the field's data type
- A `NOT` toggle to negate the condition.

Conditions are combined using `AND` or `OR` between rows. They can also be
grouped using parentheses to control the order of evaluation.

Below is a text-based mock-up of the user interface based on the Flora data
package example used in the step above:

```text
[ + Add condition ] [ + Add group ]

Group: OR --------------------------------------------------------------
[ NOT ✗ ] [common_name ⏷ ] [ == ⏷ ] [ "rose" ]                [ 🗑️ ]
[ NOT ✗ ] [common_name ⏷ ] [ == ⏷ ] [ "tulip" ]               [ 🗑️ ]

[ + Add condition to group ]
------------------------------------------------------------------------

AND ⏷ 

[ NOT ✗ ] [ country ⏷ ] [ == ⏷ ] [ "Denmark" ]                [ 🗑️ ]

AND ⏷

[ NOT ✓ ] [ observation_date ⏷ ] [ before ⏷ ] [ 2020-01-01 ]  [ 🗑️ ]
```

<!-- TODO: Add a button to delete everything within a group? -->
<!-- TODO: Do we want to add a live preview of the query below this? -->
<!-- TODO: Should there be a check/warning if a filter returns 0 rows? -->

The plus, `+`, denotes the possibility to add a condition or group, the arrow
down symbol, `⏷` denotes a dropdown menu, and the waste bin symbol, `[ 🗑️ ]`,
denotes the potential to delete the condition. The x, `✗`, and checkmark, `✓`
after `NOT` conditions denote whether the toggle is active.

## Step 5: Review request

In this step, the entirety of the request is shown, from Steps 2 to 4. It's not
possible for the Requester to edit the request on this page (to edit they need
to go back to the previous step(s)), but this is a chance to review the entire
request and confirm that it is ready to be sent to an Owner.

## Step 6: Save `request.yaml` and/or send it to Owner

After the request has been reviewed, the Requester can save it locally and/or
send it to the Owner. At this step, the Requester is shown the contact
information of the Owner (potentially Owners) and they can select which Owner to
send the request to. When Requesters click the "Send" button at the Owner's
email address, Propagate opens the Requester's email app with the "To" field
populated with the Owner's address and the `request.yaml` attached.

<!-- TODO: Do we want to add some information in the request yaml file name? E.g., datetime or project name? -->

Below is a text-based mock-up of how the Owner(s) are shown:

```text
| Name          | Email                       |          |
|---------------|-----------------------------|----------|
| Jamie Johnson | <jamie.johnson@example.org> | [ Send ] |
| Joey Jensen   | <joey.jensen@example.org>   | [ Send ] |

[ ⬇ Download request.yaml ] 
```

After the request has been sent to the Owner by email, the remaining steps
(e.g., whether or not the Owner approves the request) are outside of the web
application's scope. If or when the request is approved, the Owner uses the
[CLI](/docs/design/interface/cli.qmd) to extract the data subset.
